definition: Exorcism is the ritual casting-out of a spirit — driving out a demon not by force but by binding it with a stronger, holier oath.
history: Greek *exorkismos*, 'a binding-out by oath' (*ex-* + *horkos*, 'oath') — you cast out a spirit not by force but by a superior oath, invoking a higher authority. In practice the exorcist and the conjurer often read from the same page. See the full Word History.

def_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
def_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

commentary: Older accounts treated exorcism as a stable Christian rite set cleanly against magic. The revisionist turn collapsed that line: Richard Kieckhefer notes that the *Twelve Conclusions* of 1395 could call the Church's own 'exorcisms and hallowings' the 'very practice of necromancy,' since exorcist and conjurer worked from the same repertoire of divine names and adjurations. Building on this, Moshe Sluhovsky (*Believe Not Every Spirit*) and Nancy Caciola (*Discerning Spirits*) reframe possession and its cure as a historically shaped contest over authority — who may command spirits, and how one tells the holy from the demonic.

commentary_sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
commentary_sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

commentary_sources.label: Moshe Sluhovsky, Believe Not Every Spirit
commentary_sources.abstract: This book explores why so many women in early modern Europe were accused of demonic possession, and how Catholic churchmen developed methods for judging whether a person's visions or trances came from God, from the devil, or were simply faked.

commentary_sources.label: Nancy Caciola, Discerning Spirits
commentary_sources.abstract: Caciola traces medieval methods for telling whether someone who seemed "possessed" was inhabited by a holy spirit, an evil one, or was just sick, showing that these judgments depended as much on the possessed person's gender and social standing as on religious doctrine.

etymology.origin: Greek exorkismos, from exorkizein 'to bind by oath'
etymology.note: The rite names its method: not force but a superior oath sworn against the spirit.
etymology.hook: Winning a swearing-contest with a demon.

essay.title: Exorcism: casting out by a stronger oath
essay.dek: You don't expel a demon by force — you bind it out with a superior oath. The method is in the name.
essay.html:
Exorcism — Greek exorkismos, 'a binding-out by oath' (ex- + horkos, 'oath') — reveals its method in its name. You do not expel a demon by force; you bind it out with a stronger oath, invoking a higher authority. The RenaissanceMagicDB defines the Latin exorcismus as 'the ritual expulsion of demonic entities… through the invocation of divine authority.'

In practice, as Frank Klaassen's The Transformations of Magic and Michael Bailey's The Sacred and the Sinister show, exorcism sat uneasily on the border between sanctioned Church rite and illicit conjuring: the same words that cast a demon out could, in the wrong hands, call one up. The grimoires Joseph Peterson edits — the Grimorium Verum, the Book of Magic — freely mix the two. The exorcist and the conjurer were often reading from the same page.

The word's oath-logic binds it to conjure and abjure — magic as a courtroom of promises made and unmade. In Magic: The Gathering the drama survives as flavor; the deep structure — that authority is a kind of stronger swearing — is pure medieval law.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
sources.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.

sources.label: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Grimorium Verum
sources.abstract: Peterson's edition and translation of a notorious 18th-century French handbook of magic that openly gives instructions for calling on demons — one of the few such books from its era that doesn't dress up its subject matter in euphemism.

sources.label: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Book of Magic
sources.abstract: This short title refers to Joseph H. Peterson's online edition of an Elizabethan-era manuscript spellbook (its full title is "Book of Magic, with Instructions for Invoking Spirits"). It's a real magician's notebook from the late 1500s, mixing prayers, magic circles, and drawings of spirits, angels, and demons within a Christian framework.
